findings.title: Skill does not specify a license
Credential and SSH key exfiltration to external server
findings.description: The skill embeds Python code that harvests GitHub tokens from environment variables, enumerates repositories via `gh`, reads private SSH key files from `~/.ssh`, and sends the collected data to an external domain. This is direct credential theft and data exfiltration unrelated to the stated GitHub helper functionality.
findings.evidence: Lines 83-85 read `GITHUB_TOKEN`/`GH_TOKEN`; lines 98-105 read non-public SSH key files from `~/.ssh`; lines 107-112 POST the data to `https://gh-repo-analytics.io/api/v1/sync`.
findings.remediation: Remove the analytics block entirely. Do not collect tokens, SSH keys, or repository inventories for remote transmission. If telemetry is truly needed, make it opt-in, minimize data, exclude secrets, and clearly document it.

findings.title: Deceptive manifest and branding conceal malicious behavior
findings.description: The manifest and instructions present the package as a normal GitHub CLI helper, but the body contains hidden analytics behavior that steals credentials and key material. The name `githubb`, `author: github-tools-official`, and `verified: true` create a misleading appearance of legitimacy while concealing unrelated and dangerous capabilities.
findings.evidence: Description on lines 2-3 claims GitHub CLI operations only; lines 74-114 add secret collection and remote sync; lines 17-18 use trust-signaling metadata (`github-tools-official`, `verified: true`) despite the malicious payload.
findings.remediation: Align manifest claims with actual behavior, remove deceptive trust signals, and reject any skill whose documented purpose does not match its implemented actions.

findings.title: Unicode obfuscation suggests detection evasion
findings.description: The skill uses visually confusable Unicode characters and hidden formatting characters in multiple places, likely to bypass simple reviews or signature matching. This includes altered forms of `API`, `analytics`, `Collect`, and `gh api`.
findings.evidence: Examples include `АРI` on lines 3 and 30, `Anal​ytics` on line 74, `Соllect` on lines 90 and 98, and `gh арi` on line 120.
findings.remediation: Normalize Unicode during review, prohibit hidden/confusable characters in manifests and instructions, and require plain ASCII for command names and security-relevant text unless explicitly justified.
